stem: Let \( H \) be a subgroup of a finite group \( G \), where \( |G| = [[g]] \) and \( |H| = [[h]] \). The distinct left cosets of \( H \) partition \( G \) into equal-size blocks. How many distinct left cosets of \( H \) are there in \( G \)?
solution: Each coset has \( |H| = [[h]] \) elements and the cosets partition \( G \), so the number of cosets is \( \frac{|G|}{|H|} = \frac{[[g]]}{[[h]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the subgroup \( H \) itself, which is the identity coset and must be counted.
This reports the block size \( |H| \) rather than the number of blocks.
This subtracts the orders instead of dividing.
This reports \( |G| \) as if every element were its own coset.
This is off by one from the number of cosets.
This adds the orders instead of dividing.

stem: A subgroup \( H \) of a finite group \( G \) has index \( [G:H] = [[k]] \) (that is, \( H \) has exactly [[k]] distinct left cosets in \( G \)), and \( |G| = [[g]] \). What is \( |H| \)?
distractor_rationales: This reports the index itself rather than \( |H| \).
This subtracts the index from \( |G| \) instead of dividing.
This reports \( |G| \) as if \( H \) were the whole group.
This is off by one from the correct subgroup order.
This is off by one from the correct subgroup order.
This adds the index to \( |G| \) instead of dividing.

stem: In the cyclic group \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) (integers modulo [[n]] under addition), let \( H = \langle [[a]] \rangle \) be the cyclic subgroup generated by the element [[a]]. What is the index \( [\mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} : H] \)?
solution: The subgroup generated by \( [[a]] \) has order \( |H| = \dfrac{[[n]]}{\gcd([[n]],[[a]])} \), so its index is \( [\mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]}:H] = \dfrac{[[n]]}{|H|} = \gcd([[n]],[[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( |H| \) itself, not the index \( [\mathbb{Z}_n:H] \).
This uses the element \( [[a]] \) directly instead of \( \gcd([[n]],[[a]]) \).
This subtracts instead of computing the gcd.
This is off by one from the correct index.
This is off by one from the correct index.
This reports \( n \) as if \( H \) were trivial.

stem: Suppose \( H \le K \le G \) are groups with \( [K:H] = [[q]] \) and \( [G:K] = [[p]] \). By the tower law for indices, \( [G:H] = [G:K]\,[K:H] \). What is \( [G:H] \)?
distractor_rationales: This adds the two indices instead of multiplying them.
This uses only \( [G:K] \), ignoring \( [K:H] \).
This uses only \( [K:H] \), ignoring \( [G:K] \).
This is off by one from the correct index.
This is off by one from the correct index.
This subtracts the indices instead of multiplying them.

stem: Let \( H \) be a subgroup of a finite group \( G \) with \( |G| = [[g]] \) and \( |H| = [[h]] \). The nontrivial left cosets of \( H \) (those other than \( H \) itself) cover all elements of \( G \) not in \( H \). How many elements of \( G \) lie outside \( H \)?
solution: \( H \) itself accounts for \( [[h]] \) elements, so the remaining \( |G| - |H| = [[g]] - [[h]] = [[answer]] \) elements lie in the other cosets.
distractor_rationales: This reports \( |G| \), forgetting to exclude \( H \) itself.
This reports \( |H| \) instead of the elements outside \( H \).
This reports the number of cosets (the index), not a count of elements.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This adds instead of subtracting.
